text: A loop of ileum is visible, densely adherent and possibly forming a fistula to the right lower quadrant anterior abdominal wall. The surgical port is placed one finger breadth above the pubic bone in the midline through a two and a half centimeter transverse incision using a mini fan and steel.
The surgeon elevates the iliocolic pedicle and performs a medial to lateral dissection to access the root of the pedicle, using careful manipulation to separate it from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon creates a window in the mesentery and prepares to take the ileocolic pedicle, using electrocautery to prevent bleeding due to the challenging angles and instrumentation of the single port laparoscopic approach.

The surgeon completes the division of the ileocolic pedicle and performs a medial to lateral dissection, followed by a lateral dissection. The goal is to mobilize the colon around the hepatic flexure to exteriorize the specimen for hand-sewn extracorporeal anastomosis through the fan and steel incision.

The surgeon checks for the ureter, which is visible and moving in the surgical field. With a favorable body habitus, the ureter is easily identified. The surgeon then begins lateral mobilization for a straightforward primary ileocolic resection.
The surgeon mobilizes the root of the terminal ileum (TI) upwards towards the duodenum, working underneath the ileocolic pedicle to facilitate the small bowel's reach to the port.
The surgeon utilizes a single port access device, employing two instruments: the Le Goucher and the Parkes Grasper, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon alternates between dissection techniques, clearing smoke from the camera by pulling it into the port. The dissection progresses to join the previously medially dissected space.

The surgeon continues to mobilize the colon around the hepatic flexure to gain length, aiming to exteriorize the cecum. The procedure is performed using a suprapubic single port approach, suitable for patients with favorable body habitus.
The surgeon mobilizes the hepatic flexure, applying tension to the surrounding tissues to facilitate the procedure.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the hepatic flexure, ensuring sufficient length for a hand-sewn anastomosis. The procedure is performed using a single port access device with visible instruments, including the Le Goucher and Parkes Grasper.
The hepatic flexure is fully mobilized and brought down, allowing the surgeon to perform a hand-sewn anastomosis. The bowel is exteriorized for this procedure.
